title: Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil

seo.title: Authentic Focaccia Genovese Recipe with Rosemary & Olive Oil
seo.metaDescription: Master the art of authentic Focaccia Genovese! This easy recipe features fragrant rosemary, rich olive oil, and a perfectly chewy, airy crumb. Bake yours today!
seo.h1: Authentic Focaccia Genovese Recipe with Rosemary & Olive Oil
h2Headings: Ingredients You'll Need for Focaccia Genovese
Expert Tips for Perfect Focaccia Genovese
Variations and Substitutions for Focaccia Genovese
seo.imageAlt: A close-up of freshly baked Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil, golden brown and dimpled.
seo.imageCaption: Golden, dimpled, and fragrant – this homemade Focaccia Genovese with rosemary and olive oil is a taste of Italy.

description: Imagine sinking your teeth into a warm slice of Focaccia Genovese, its golden crust crackling slightly to reveal a soft, airy interior. This authentic recipe brings the fragrant aroma of rosemary and the rich taste of olive oil straight from the Ligurian coast to your kitchen. Perfect as an appetizer, a side, or simply enjoyed on its own, this focaccia is a true Italian masterpiece.

ingredients.notes: plus more for drizzling and pan

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a large bowl, combine the warm water and yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy. This indicates the yeast is active and ready.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dough
instructions.text: Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Mix with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms. Drizzle in 2 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil and continue to mix until combined. The dough will be sticky.

instructions.title: First Rise and Kneading
instructions.text: Lightly oil a clean bowl. Transfer the dough to the oiled bowl, turning once to coat. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp towel and let it rise in a warm place for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until doubled in size. Then, gently punch down the dough.

instructions.title: Shape the Focaccia
instructions.text: Pour 2 tablespoons of olive oil into a 9x13 inch baking pan, ensuring the bottom and sides are well coated. Transfer the dough to the pan and gently stretch it to fill the pan, being careful not to tear it. If it resists, let it rest for 10 minutes and try again.

instructions.title: Second Rise and Seasoning
instructions.text: Cover the pan loosely with plastic wrap and let the dough rise again for 30-45 minutes, or until noticeably puffy. Preheat your oven to 425F (220C). Once risen, use your fingertips to create deep dimples all over the surface of the dough. Drizzle generously with the remaining olive oil, sprinkle with chopped rosemary and coarse sea salt.

instructions.title: Bake to Golden Perfection
instructions.text: Bake the focaccia in the preheated oven for 25-35 minutes, or until it is golden brown and cooked through. The crust should be crisp and the interior soft. Remove from the oven and let it cool slightly in the pan before transferring to a wire rack to cool completely. Serve warm or at room temperature.

tips: For an extra airy focaccia, use a high hydration dough. This recipe already has a good ratio, but avoid adding too much extra flour during mixing.
Do not skip the dimpling step! It creates those characteristic pockets that hold the olive oil and seasonings, preventing large air bubbles.
Focaccia is best enjoyed fresh on the day it is baked. Store leftovers tightly wrapped at room temperature for up to 2 days, or freeze slices for longer storage.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the sun-drenched coast of Liguria, Italy, where a simple yet sublime bread reigns supreme: Focaccia Genovese. More than just a flatbread, this iconic Italian baking masterpiece is a testament to the power of quality ingredients and time-honored techniques. Imagine a golden-crusted slab of airy, olive oil-rich dough, studded with fragrant rosemary and coarse sea salt, begging to be torn and savored. This isn't just any focaccia; it's the Genovese variety, distinct for its thin, crispy bottom, soft, airy crumb, and generous, almost excessive, use of exceptional extra virgin olive oil. If you're eager to bring a taste of Genoa into your kitchen, and you're looking for the ultimate Focaccia Genovese Recipe, you've come to the right place. Get ready to discover the secrets behind this beloved Italian staple, from its humble origins to the expert tips that will elevate your baking.

## What is Focaccia Genovese?

Focaccia Genovese, or "fugassa" in the local Ligurian dialect, is a traditional flatbread originating from Genoa, the capital city of the Liguria region in northwest Italy. Unlike its thicker, often more bread-like cousins found elsewhere, Genovese focaccia is characterized by its relatively thin profile, incredibly tender and airy interior, and a uniquely dimpled surface. These dimples, pressed into the dough before baking, serve a crucial purpose: they act as small wells to hold pools of extra virgin olive oil, which then caramelizes slightly in the hot oven, contributing to its signature crispy crust and rich flavor.

Historically, focaccia was a humble food, often baked in the ashes of a fire or in wood-fired ovens. It served as a quick snack, a breakfast item dipped in coffee, or an accompaniment to meals. The Genovese version stands out due to its high hydration dough, which leads to its exceptionally light texture, and its reliance on the finest local olive oil. The combination of simple ingredients – flour, water, yeast, salt, olive oil, and rosemary – transforms into something truly extraordinary, embodying the essence of Ligurian cuisine. It's a testament to the fact that sometimes, less truly is more, especially when the quality of each component is paramount.

## Ingredients You'll Need for Focaccia Genovese

Crafting an authentic Focaccia Genovese requires a few essential ingredients, with quality being key, especially for the olive oil. Here's what you'll need to gather for this delightful Focaccia Genovese Recipe:

*   **Strong Bread Flour:** About 500g (around 4 cups). Bread flour, with its higher protein content, develops more gluten, giving the focaccia its characteristic chew and structure. All-purpose flour can work in a pinch, but bread flour is preferred.
*   **Warm Water:** Around 350-400ml (1.5 to 1.75 cups). The exact amount can vary based on flour absorption, so start with less and add more if needed. It should be lukewarm, not hot, to activate the yeast.
*   **Active Dry Yeast:** 7g (about 2 teaspoons). This is the leavening agent that gives the focaccia its rise and airy texture.
*   **Fine Sea Salt:** 10g (about 1.5 teaspoons) for the dough, plus extra coarse sea salt for sprinkling on top.
*   **Granulated Sugar (optional):** A pinch (about 1/2 teaspoon) to help activate the yeast.
*   **High-Quality Extra Virgin Olive Oil:** At least 100-150ml (about 1/2 to 2/3 cup), plus extra for oiling the pan and drizzling. This is non-negotiable for authentic Focaccia Genovese. Use the best you can find, preferably a Ligurian or Italian EVOO.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** A few sprigs, destemmed and roughly chopped, for sprinkling on top. Dried rosemary can be used, but fresh offers superior aroma and flavor.

These simple ingredients, when combined with care and attention, lay the foundation for an unforgettable Italian baking experience.

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Focaccia Genovese

Here’s a detailed Focaccia Genovese Recipe to guide you through the process, ensuring a perfect loaf every time:

1.  **Activate the Yeast:** In a large bowl, combine the warm water, yeast, and optional pinch of sugar. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until foamy, indicating the yeast is active.
2.  **Mix the Dough:** Add the flour and fine sea salt to the yeast mixture. Stir with a wooden spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms.
3.  **Knead (or No-Knead Method):**
    *   **Traditional Kneading:** Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 8-10 minutes until it's smooth, elastic, and passes the windowpane test (you can stretch a small piece thin enough to see light through it without tearing).
    *   **No-Knead (for a softer crumb):** If you prefer a less hands-on approach, simply cover the bowl and let it rest for 15 minutes. Then, using wet hands, perform a series of "stretch and folds" in the bowl. Grab a portion of the dough, stretch it upwards, and fold it over itself. Rotate the bowl and repeat this 4-5 times. Cover and repeat this process every 30 minutes for a total of 2-3 times. This develops gluten without traditional kneading.
4.  **First Rise (Bulk Fermentation):** Lightly grease the mixing bowl with a tablespoon of olive oil. Place the dough in the bowl, turning it once to coat. Cover the bowl with plastic wrap or a damp towel and let it rise in a warm place for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until doubled in size.
5.  **Prepare the Pan:** Liberally coat a 9x13-inch (or similar size) baking pan with 3-4 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil. Make sure the oil covers the entire bottom and sides.
6.  **Transfer and Stretch:** Gently deflate the risen dough and carefully transfer it to the oiled baking pan. Drizzle a little more olive oil on top of the dough. Using your fingertips, gently press and stretch the dough to fill the pan. Be patient; if it springs back, let it rest for 10-15 minutes, then continue stretching. You want an even thickness.
7.  **Second Rise (Proofing):** Once stretched, cover the pan lightly with plastic wrap or a damp towel and let it proof for another 30-45 minutes in a warm place. The dough should look puffy and soft.
8.  **The Dimples and Toppings:** Preheat your oven to 220°C (425°F). With well-oiled fingertips, gently but firmly press deep indentations all over the surface of the dough, making sure to avoid tearing it. These dimples should go almost all the way down to the bottom of the pan. Drizzle generously with another 3-4 tablespoons of extra virgin olive oil, ensuring it pools in the dimples. Sprinkle evenly with the chopped fresh rosemary and coarse sea salt.
9.  **Bake:** Place the pan in the preheated oven and bake for 20-30 minutes, or until the focaccia is golden brown on top and the bottom is crispy and golden. The exact time depends on your oven.
10. **Cool:** Once baked, immediately transfer the Focaccia Genovese from the pan to a wire rack to cool slightly. This prevents the bottom from becoming soggy.
11. **Serve:** Cut into squares or strips and serve warm. Following this Focaccia Genovese Recipe will ensure a truly authentic experience.

## Expert Tips for Perfect Focaccia Genovese

Mastering this Focaccia Genovese Recipe requires attention to a few key details that elevate it from good to truly exceptional:

*   **Olive Oil is King:** We cannot stress this enough. Invest in a high-quality, flavorful extra virgin olive oil. It’s not just a fat; it’s a primary flavor component. Use it generously in the pan, in the dough, and especially on top before baking. The pools of oil in the dimples are what give Genovese focaccia its unique texture and taste.
*   **High Hydration Dough:** Genovese focaccia dough is typically very wet and sticky. Don't be tempted to add too much flour during mixing or kneading. This high hydration is crucial for the airy, open crumb structure. Embrace the stickiness!
*   **Gentle Dimpling:** When creating the dimples, use oiled fingertips and be firm but gentle. You want to press down deeply to create wells for the olive oil without deflating the dough completely. Avoid tearing the dough.
*   **Don't Skimp on Proofing Time:** Both the first and second rises are essential for developing flavor and texture. A warm environment helps, but don't rush the process. A slow, steady rise yields better results.
*   **Coarse Sea Salt:** Use coarse sea salt, not fine table salt, for the topping. The larger crystals provide bursts of salty flavor and a delightful textural contrast.
*   **Hot Oven:** A hot oven (220°C / 425°F) is vital for achieving that perfect golden-brown, crispy crust while keeping the interior soft and moist.
*   **Fresh Rosemary:** While dried rosemary can be used, fresh rosemary provides a far superior aroma and flavor, making a significant difference to the overall experience.
*   **Cool on a Rack:** Always transfer the focaccia to a wire rack immediately after baking. Leaving it in the hot pan can trap steam and make the bottom soggy.

## Serving Suggestions for Your Focaccia

Once you've perfected your Focaccia Genovese Recipe, the possibilities for enjoying it are endless. This versatile bread is a star in its own right, but also a fantastic companion:

*   **Breakfast or Snack:** In Liguria, it's traditional to enjoy focaccia for breakfast, often dipped in cappuccino or coffee. It's also a perfect mid-morning or afternoon snack.
*   **Appetizer:** Serve warm slices with a simple dip of extra virgin olive oil and balsamic vinegar, alongside olives, cured meats, and cheeses for an authentic Italian antipasto spread.
*   **Sandwich Base:** Its sturdy yet soft texture makes it an ideal base for sandwiches. Fill it with prosciutto, mortadella, fresh mozzarella, ripe tomatoes, basil, or grilled vegetables.
*   **Accompaniment to Meals:** It pairs beautifully with soups, stews, salads, or any main course that benefits from a delicious bread for dipping or sopping up sauces.
*   **Bruschetta Alternative:** Top toasted pieces with fresh tomatoes, garlic, and basil for a twist on classic bruschetta.

## Variations and Substitutions for Focaccia Genovese

While the classic Focaccia Genovese Rosemary Olive Oil is perfect as is, you can explore slight variations once you've mastered the basic Focaccia Genovese Recipe:

*   **Other Herbs:** While rosemary is traditional for the Genovese style, you could experiment with fresh thyme, oregano, or a mix of Italian herbs for a different aromatic profile.
*   **Added Toppings:** For a departure from the classic, consider adding thinly sliced onions, cherry tomatoes, black olives, or even a sprinkle of Parmesan cheese before baking. Remember, these will make it a "focaccia with toppings" rather than strictly "Focaccia Genovese," which is usually minimalist.
*   **Gluten-Free Flour:** For those with gluten sensitivities, a high-quality gluten-free bread flour blend can be used. Be aware that the texture will be different, often denser, as gluten is crucial for the classic focaccia's chewiness and airiness.
*   **Sweet Focaccia:** While less common for Genovese style, some regions make sweet focaccia with sugar, raisins, or even candied fruit. This is a significant departure but highlights the versatility of the dough.

Embracing the simplicity and quality of ingredients is key to the true Focaccia Genovese experience. While variations are fun, we highly recommend starting with and perfecting the classic rosemary and olive oil version to truly appreciate its understated brilliance.

Armed with this comprehensive Focaccia Genovese Recipe, you're now ready to recreate a beloved piece of Italian culinary heritage in your own kitchen. The aroma of baking focaccia, rich with olive oil and rosemary, will transport you straight to the bustling streets and sun-drenched coastlines of Genoa. Enjoy the process, savor the results, and delight in the simple pleasure of authentic Italian baking. Buon appetito!
